title: Master Closet System Drawers
summary: Faceless build-to-fit drawer boxes for a closet system, on cheap Euro slides. No fixed dimensions — you measure your opening and build to suit. A skills plan, not a cut list.
description:
This is less a fixed project and more a method: how to add functional, faceless drawers to a master closet built-in, using inexpensive white Euro drawer slides. Because closet openings vary, there's no universal cut list here — the whole point is building to fit your actual opening. That's the plan's central lesson, and it's the right one: drawers are precise, board widths drift by a hair, and a drawer built to a number instead of to the real opening is a drawer that binds.

Two things make or break closet drawers. First, the cabinet or opening must be square — measure the inside width at top and bottom and confirm they match before you build anything. Second, build each drawer box to the measured opening minus the slide clearance the hardware calls for (typically 1/2" per side for these Euro slides). In a closet you can skip drawer faces entirely — the source builder left the slides and 1/2" gaps visible on all four sides, because it's a closet, and that's completely fine.

Installing the slides is the fussy part: decide where each drawer bottom sits, add clearance to the top, draw a square line for the cabinet-side member on both walls, and install top-down one drawer at a time. These cupped-bottom Euro slides also hold the drawer bottom in place, so you don't need fancy joinery to attach it. Because this is build-to-fit, the cut list is intentionally left empty — dimensions come from your opening. Materials are given as a shopping guide.

tools.note: Square lines for the slide members on both walls.
The most important tool here — measure the actual opening.

materials.name: 1x8 boards (drawer sides)
materials.note: Any width works; height depends on your drawer depth.

materials.name: White Euro drawer slides
materials.note: In a width matching your opening; these cup the drawer bottom.

steps.title: Measure the opening and confirm square
steps.body: The two biggest ways to get closet drawers wrong are a cabinet that isn't square and not building to fit. Start by measuring the inside width of your closet opening at top and bottom — they should match. Board widths can be off by a hair, and that throws everything off, so measure the real opening, don't assume a number.

steps.title: Build the drawer boxes to fit
steps.body: Build each drawer box to fit the opening minus the slide clearance your hardware needs. In a closet you can skip full faces entirely — leave the slides and the 1/2" gaps visible on all four sides. It's a closet; function beats a finished face here.

steps.title: Install the cabinet-side slide members
steps.body: Decide where each drawer bottom sits. For standard drawers, add 1/8" at the top for clearance plus the drawer's overall height to locate the box bottom. Draw a square line on both walls so you know it's square to the cabinet, and install the cabinet-side slide members with their long straight edge on the line. Work one drawer at a time, top-down.

steps.title: Attach the drawer members and hang the drawers
steps.body: Attach the drawer-side members to the drawer boxes. These slides cup the bottom corner of the box, which also keeps the drawer bottom attached — so you don't need fancy joinery to hold the bottom in. Hang each drawer and check it glides.

steps.title: Add knobs and finish
steps.body: Add knobs or handles, sand, and finish. If you end up with a little space at the bottom, it's a perfect spot for a few pairs of shoes.

images.alt: Faceless build-to-fit drawers installed in a closet system
